source_paper_title: Near-Optimal Performance of Stochastic Model Predictive Control
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Shin_2026, title={Near-Optimal Performance of Stochastic Model Predictive Control}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0159}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0159}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Shin, Sungho and Na, Sen and Anitescu, Mihai}, year={2026}, month=jan }
context:
Page 7, Remark 5; and page 13, Section 4 (Conclusions and Future Work), third bullet.

Remark 5. One limitation of our analysis is the assumption that the exact distribution of uncertainties is known. In practice, the true distribution of these uncertainties may remain unknown, highlighting the importance of incorporating distributional robustness into the control policy. Recent work in a different context has demonstrated stability in expectation within the stochastic MPC framework when the Wasserstein distance between the true and nominal distributions is bounded [45]. Nevertheless, the dynamic regret analysis regarding the distributional robustness property remains an open question.

Analyzing dynamic regret under inexact knowledge of the distribution presents an intriguing avenue for future research.
title: Dynamic regret bounds for distributionally robust stochastic model predictive control policies
background_latex:
Consider the finite-horizon stochastic control problem over stages \(t=0,1,\dots,T\) with random disturbances \(\xi:=\{\xi_t\}_{t=0}^T\). The system evolves according to linear dynamics with additive and multiplicative uncertainty
\[
 x_t = A(\xi_t)x_{t-1}+B(\xi_t)u_{t-1}+d(\xi_t),
\]
and incurs a (possibly random) quadratic stage cost
\[
\ell(x_t,u_t;\xi_t)=\tfrac12\begin{bmatrix}x_t\\u_t\end{bmatrix}^\top
\begin{bmatrix}Q(\xi_t)&0\\0&R(\xi_t)\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}x_t\\u_t\end{bmatrix}-
\begin{bmatrix}q(\xi_t)\\r(\xi_t)\end{bmatrix}^\top\begin{bmatrix}x_t\\u_t\end{bmatrix}.
\]
A (nonanticipative) control policy chooses \(u_t\) after observing the history \(\xi_{0:t}\). Let \(J^\star(\xi_0;w_{-1})\) denote the optimal expected total cost over \(t=0,\dots,T\) under the true distribution of \(\xi\), given initial augmented state-control \(w_{-1}=(x_{-1},u_{-1})\) and initial uncertainty \(\xi_0\).

Stochastic model predictive control (SMPC) with prediction horizon \(W\) constructs a closed-loop policy by repeatedly solving, at each stage \(\tau\), a truncated-horizon stochastic optimal control problem over the next \(W\) stages using a (nominal) conditional distribution of future disturbances given \(\xi_{0:\tau}\), then applying only the first control action and re-optimizing at the next stage. Let \(J^{(W)}(\xi_0;w_{-1})\) denote the expected total cost incurred by the resulting SMPC closed-loop policy.

The dynamic regret of the SMPC policy is
\[
\mathrm{Regret}(W):=J^{(W)}(\xi_0;w_{-1})-J^\star(\xi_0;w_{-1}).
\]
In the paper's main results, dynamic regret bounds are proved under the assumption that the distribution of \(\xi\) is exactly known (and, for the main analysis, has finite support so that a scenario tree representation is available).

In practice the true distribution of \(\xi\) may be unknown. A distributionally robust variant of SMPC would design the truncated problems using a nominal distribution \(\widehat{\mathbb P}\) but aim to guarantee performance under an unknown true distribution \(\mathbb P\) belonging to some ambiguity set \(\mathcal P\) (for example, a Wasserstein ball around \(\widehat{\mathbb P}\)). Let \(J_{\mathbb P}^\star\) and \(J_{\mathbb P}^{(W)}\) denote the optimal and SMPC expected costs when expectations are taken under \(\mathbb P\).
problem_definition_latex:
Develop a dynamic regret analysis for SMPC when the disturbance distribution is not known exactly, i.e., obtain bounds (or rates) on
\[
J_{\mathbb P}^{(W)}(\xi_0;w_{-1})-J_{\mathbb P}^\star(\xi_0;w_{-1})
\]
under inexact knowledge of the distribution of \(\xi\) (for instance, in a distributionally robust setting where \(\mathbb P\) is only known to belong to an ambiguity set \(\mathcal P\)).

location_in_paper: Page 7, Remark 5; and page 13, Section 4 (Conclusions and Future Work), third bullet.
open_problem_quote:
Remark 5. One limitation of our analysis is the assumption that the exact distribution of uncertainties is known. In practice, the true distribution of these uncertainties may remain unknown, highlighting the importance of incorporating distributional robustness into the control policy. Recent work in a different context has demonstrated stability in expectation within the stochastic MPC framework when the Wasserstein distance between the true and nominal distributions is bounded [45]. Nevertheless, the dynamic regret analysis regarding the distributional robustness property remains an open question.

Analyzing dynamic regret under inexact knowledge of the distribution presents an intriguing avenue for future research.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can computationally search for instances where a candidate robust SMPC design exhibits large regret under adversarial distributions within a chosen ambiguity set, since the regret can be estimated via scenario simulation. However, the open question is about proving general bounds, and selecting a faithful ambiguity model and worst-case evaluation makes automated counterexample search only moderately well-posed.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate robust SMPC schemes (e.g., choices of ambiguity sets, penalties, or robust-horizon parameters) can be encoded and evaluated by Monte Carlo estimation of regret over sampled dynamics and distributions, providing an automatic fitness signal. Still, the target is a universal theoretical bound, and simulation-based fitness may not correlate reliably with provable guarantees, so suitability is moderate rather than high.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper proves that, under finite-support disturbances and stabilizability/detectability (Assumption 2), the dynamic regret of scenario-tree SMPC decays exponentially in the prediction horizon \(W\): \(J^{(W)}-J^\star \le \mathrm{poly}(T,\|w_{-1}\|,D)\,\rho^W\). Remark 5 explicitly flags the missing piece for practice: the true disturbance law \(\mathbb P\) is unknown, so SMPC is implemented with a nominal conditional law \(\widehat{\mathbb P}\) or an ambiguity set \(\mathcal P\), and one would like bounds on \(J^{(W)}_{\mathbb P}-J^\star_{\mathbb P}\) that degrade gracefully with distribution mismatch.

Among closely related works, the strongest distributional-robustness results for MPC currently concern stability/trajectory bounds rather than regret. In particular, "On the Inherent Distributional Robustness of Stochastic and Nominal Model Predictive Control" provides Wasserstein-distance-based bounds showing that closed-loop behavior changes continuously with the disturbance distribution, aligning with the robustness direction suggested in the source paper. Companion papers by McAllister and Rawlings develop the stochastic stability foundations (existence/measurability and stochastic asymptotic stability) and robustness comparisons between nominal and stochastic MPC. On the regret side, perturbation-based dynamic regret analyses for predictive control in linear time-varying systems (e.g., "Perturbation-based regret analysis of predictive control in linear time varying systems") supply the methodological template used by the source paper (KKT sensitivity decay), while online-control-with-predictions works relate performance loss to prediction error.

Overall, a full solution appears to remain open: existing distributionally robust MPC papers do not yet provide a bound of the form \(J^{(W)}_{\mathbb P}-J^\star_{\mathbb P}\le \text{(terms)}\cdot \rho^W + \text{(terms depending on }d(\mathbb P,\widehat{\mathbb P})\text{)}\). A promising direction is to combine (i) the source paper’s scenario-tree KKT perturbation machinery (probability-scaled norms and exponential decay on tree-structured KKT systems) with (ii) Wasserstein-robust stability/continuity estimates that control how the truncated-horizon objective and its gradients change under distributional perturbations. This would likely require new Lipschitz-in-distribution bounds for the conditional expectation operators appearing in the truncated problems, and a careful separation of (a) truncation error (handled by \(\rho^W\)) and (b) distributional-modeling error (handled by the ambiguity radius).

sources.title: On the Inherent Distributional Robustness of Stochastic and Nominal Model Predictive Control
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes distributional robustness (stability/trajectory bounds) for nominal and stochastic MPC under Wasserstein ambiguity: closed-loop state bounds depend on the Wasserstein distance between the true and nominal disturbance distributions.
sources.connection: Provides the closest existing distributionally robust SMPC guarantee in the same spirit as Remark 5 of the source paper, but it addresses stability/robustness rather than dynamic regret (performance gap to the \,\(\mathbb P\)-optimal policy).

sources.title: The Stochastic Robustness of Nominal and Stochastic Model Predictive Control
sources.theorem_or_result: Analyzes robustness properties of nominal vs stochastic MPC, including how closed-loop behavior changes under stochastic disturbances; develops stochastic robustness notions and bounds.
sources.connection: Supplies technical stability/robustness machinery that a dynamic-regret-under-ambiguity analysis would likely need, but does not provide regret bounds relative to the \,\(\mathbb P\)-optimal policy.

sources.title: Nonlinear Stochastic Model Predictive Control: Existence, Measurability, and Stochastic Asymptotic Stability
sources.theorem_or_result: Provides foundational results for nonlinear SMPC: existence/measurability of policies and stochastic asymptotic stability in expectation under standard MPC assumptions.
sources.connection: Gives stability foundations for SMPC beyond the linear-quadratic setting of the source paper; could be combined with distributional robustness ideas, but does not treat regret.

sources.title: Perturbation-based regret analysis of predictive control in linear time varying systems
sources.theorem_or_result: Proves exponential decay of dynamic regret in prediction horizon for deterministic/time-varying linear quadratic MPC via perturbation analysis of KKT systems.
sources.connection: Methodological precursor to the source paper's scenario-tree KKT perturbation analysis; suggests a route to regret bounds when the model is misspecified, but does not incorporate distributional ambiguity in expectations.

sources.title: Online optimal control with linear dynamics and predictions: Algorithms and regret analysis
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops online control algorithms with predictions and provides regret bounds relative to a dynamic benchmark; introduces techniques for handling prediction errors in linear systems.
sources.connection: Although not distributionally robust SMPC, it provides regret-analysis tools under imperfect forecasts that could be adapted to ambiguity in conditional disturbance laws.

sources.title: The power of predictions in online control
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies online control with predictions, quantifying how prediction quality affects performance; provides competitive/regret-style guarantees.
sources.connection: Offers a complementary framework to relate performance loss to prediction/estimation error, potentially translatable to distributional mismatch in SMPC.

sources.title: Input-to-state stability of robust receding horizon control with an expected value cost
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes ISS-type stability for robust receding horizon control using expected-value costs via affine disturbance feedback parameterizations (additive uncertainty).
sources.connection: Early stability analysis for stochastic/robust MPC with expected costs; relevant as a stability ingredient but does not address dynamic regret nor distributional ambiguity sets.
